text:
```json
[
  "Paged and Demand Paging memory allocation schemes eliminate the need for contiguous program storage and for entire programs to reside in memory, dividing jobs into equal-sized pages loaded non-contiguously; Paged allocation introduces internal fragmentation but prevents external fragmentation, while Demand Paging loads only necessary pages as needed, introducing virtual memory and allowing programs to exceed physical memory, though incurring overhead.",
  "The Memory Manager uses Page Map Tables (PMT) to manage page swapping and handle page faults, which are minimized by loading a job's working set and employing page replacement policies; this virtual memory capability, a cooperative effort with processor hardware, enables efficient memory utilization, unlimited multiprogramming, and sharing of code and data.",
  "Segmented memory allocation divides jobs into variable-sized, user-visible segments, preventing internal fragmentation but potentially leading to external fragmentation; the combined segmented/demand paged approach subdivides segments into pages, leveraging both methods to eliminate external and minimize internal fragmentation, despite requiring more tables and increasing overhead.",
  "To speed up address translation and mitigate the time-consuming access of Segment Map and Page Map tables, associative memory stores information about most-recently-used pages, allowing simultaneous searches and bypassing slower table lookups when a match is found."
]
```
json_data: Paged and Demand Paging memory allocation schemes eliminate the need for contiguous program storage and for entire programs to reside in memory, dividing jobs into equal-sized pages loaded non-contiguously; Paged allocation introduces internal fragmentation but prevents external fragmentation, while Demand Paging loads only necessary pages as needed, introducing virtual memory and allowing programs to exceed physical memory, though incurring overhead.

The Memory Manager uses Page Map Tables (PMT) to manage page swapping and handle page faults, which are minimized by loading a job's working set and employing page replacement policies; this virtual memory capability, a cooperative effort with processor hardware, enables efficient memory utilization, unlimited multiprogramming, and sharing of code and data.

Segmented memory allocation divides jobs into variable-sized, user-visible segments, preventing internal fragmentation but potentially leading to external fragmentation; the combined segmented/demand paged approach subdivides segments into pages, leveraging both methods to eliminate external and minimize internal fragmentation, despite requiring more tables and increasing overhead.

To speed up address translation and mitigate the time-consuming access of Segment Map and Page Map tables, associative memory stores information about most-recently-used pages, allowing simultaneous searches and bypassing slower table lookups when a match is found.
